Door het gespartel van de kinderen vertroebelde het water van wolken en we konden geen vissen meer zien.
Hij is de trotse vader van een wolk van een zoon geworden.

wolk.e: From Middle Dutch wolke, from Old Dutch wulko, from Proto-West Germanic *wolkn.

s.def: to win, to triumph, to be victorious (in)
to extract, to refine, to harvest (from base materials such as ore or crops)

winnen.e: From Middle Dutch winnen, from Old Dutch winnan, from Proto-West Germanic *winnan, from Proto-Germanic *winnaną, from Proto-Indo-European *wenh₁- (“to strive, desire, wish, love”).

wachten.e: From Middle Dutch wachten, from Old Dutch *wahton, from Proto-West Germanic *wahtwēn. Equivalent to wacht (“watch, vigil”) + -en.

wakker.e: From Middle Dutch wacker, from Old Dutch *wakkar, from Proto-West Germanic *wakr. Cognate with German wacker (“stalwart”), dialectal English waker (“vigilant”, see there).

examples: Ik heb mij wreed geamuseerd.

wreed.e: From Middle Dutch wreet, from Old Dutch *wrēth, from Proto-West Germanic *wraiþ, from Proto-Germanic *wraiþaz. Cognate with English wrath.

examples: Ik wil een ijsje.
Wil je dat ik langskom?

s.def: to want to, will, be willing to
examples: Hij wil gaan slapen.

s.def: to want to go somewhere (cf. English want in)
examples: Ze wilden het huis in.

s.def: to wish, would like, would (as in dated English I would that)
examples: Ik wou dat ik dat kon.

willen.e: From Middle Dutch willen, from Old Dutch willen, from Proto-West Germanic *willjan, from Proto-Germanic *wiljaną, from Proto-Indo-European *welh₁-. The variant past forms with ou go back to early Middle Dutch wolde(n), following the regular shift old, olt > oud, out.

examples:
1778, Thomas Harmer, Waarnemingen over het Oosten uit reisbeschrijvingen, tot opheldering der Heilige Schrift, translated from English, with comments by "Johan Ernst Faber" (= Johann Ernst Faber), translated from German, publ. by G. T. van Paddenburg, second book, page 237.
Toen vond men nog veele wouden, waarvan er tot op dezen dag nog verſcheidenen overig gebleven zijn (12). Uit zulke wouden haalden de dorpſchappen hunnen brand, wanneer zij, gelijk om Aleppo zelfs, geen houwboſſchen hadden.

s.def: mass, multitude, sea (now chiefly in relation to rules and prescriptions)
examples: een woud aan regels

s.def: a hamlet in Sint-Michielsgestel, North Brabant, Netherlands

woud.e: From Middle Dutch wout, from Old Dutch walt, from Proto-West Germanic *walþu, from Proto-Germanic *walþuz.

examples: Wanneer komt de trein?
Wat kun je als professional doen wanneer je weet of een vermoeden hebt dat je patiënt zwakbegaafd of licht verstandelijk beperkt is?
Wanneer ik dat doe, gebeurt er dit.
Wanneer hij mij ziet, loopt ie weg.

wanneer.e: From Middle Dutch wanneer, wan eer, from Old Dutch *wan ēr. The former part is found as Middle Dutch wan, from Old Dutch *wan, from Proto-West Germanic *hwan, from Proto-Germanic *hwan. The latter part is identical with modern eer (“before”). Cognate with Low German wannehr, Luxembourgish wéini, ...

waarschijnlijk.e: From waerschijnlick, from Middle Dutch schijnlic. Possibly calque of French vraisemblable or Latin vērī similis. By surface analysis, waar (“true”) + schijnen (“to appear”) + -lijk (“-ly”).

examples: Welkom in Brabant

welkom.e: Ultimately from Proto-Germanic *wiljakumô. Cognate to English welcome, West Frisian wolkom, German willkommen, Danish velkommen and Norwegian Bokmål velkommen.

examples: Waarom ben je zo laat thuisgekomen?
Ik begrijp niet waarom ze boos is.

s.def: pronominal adverb form of om + wat

waarom.e: From Middle Dutch waeromme. Equivalent to a univerbation of waar + om. Compare German warum.

s.def: to know (knowledge), to be aware of something
examples: Ik weet hoe die planeet heet.
Ik ken Rusland niet, ik weet niets over dat land.
Ik weet nog hoe koud het vorig jaar was.

s.def: to be able to, to manage to (literally, "to know how to/to know of a way to")
examples: De Batavieren wisten zich goed te verdedigen tegen de Romeinse aanval.
Deze partij wist niet eerder de kiesdrempel te halen.

weten.e: From Middle Dutch wēten, from Old Dutch witan, from Proto-West Germanic *witan, from Proto-Germanic *witaną, from Proto-Indo-European *wóyde (“know”), a root perfect from the root *weyd-. The past tense forms is also composed of the regular past tense marker -te.

s.def: to weigh (to determine the weight or be of a specified weight)
examples: Hoeveel weeg je?
Het schip was gebouwd voor een bemanning van 435 personen en woog 1200 ton.

to weigh (to evaluate, to assign value or relative importance to)

wegen.e: From Middle Dutch wēgen, from Old Dutch *wegan, from Proto-West Germanic *wegan, from Proto-Germanic *weganą, from Proto-Indo-European *wéǵʰeti.

wit-rusland.e: From wit (“white”) + Rusland (“Russia”), a calque of Russian Белору́ссия (Belorússija). Cognate with German Weißrussland, English White Russia, Faroese Hvítarussland, Swedish Vitryssland among others.

wekelijks.e: From Middle Dutch wekelijcs. Equivalent to week + -e- + -lijks.

wiskunde.e: From earlier Modern Dutch wisconst (literally “art of what is known”). Equivalent to wis + -kunde. Purism introduced as a calque of Ancient Greek μαθηματικός (mathēmatikós) by Simon Stevin in the 16th century to replace the Dutch word mathematica which was a borrowing from Latin mathēmatica.

s.def: a sled, moving platform on wheels or rails a heavy machine etc. is mounted on
a load filling one of the above vehicles

wagen.e: From Middle Dutch wāgen, from Old Dutch wagan, from Proto-West Germanic *wagn, from Proto-Germanic *wagnaz, from Proto-Indo-European *weǵʰ-. Doublet of wagon.

s.def: little, few, not much, not many
little, not much, not a lot
examples: Je hebt vandaag maar weinig gezegd. Voel je je niet goed?

weinig.e: From Middle Dutch weinich, wenich, from Old Dutch *weinag, *wēnag, from Proto-Germanic *wainagaz. Cognate with German wenig (“few”).

s.def: to cause, to elicit (particularly of certain emotions)
examples: De oplichter nam geld op zonder argwaan te wekken.

wekken.e: From Middle Dutch wecken, from Old Dutch wecken, from Proto-West Germanic *wakkjan, from Proto-Germanic *wakjaną.

worstelaar.e: From Middle Dutch worstelere. Equivalent to worstelen + -aar.

examples: kleren wringen
iemand's arm wringen
iemand iets uit de handen wringen

s.def: to wriggle, to squirm, to writhe

wringen.e: From Middle Dutch wringen, from Old Dutch *wringan, from Proto-Germanic *wringaną.

examples: Hou je waffel!

winters.e: From Middle Dutch wintersch. Equivalent to winter + -s.

wallonië.e: From Frankish *walh, from Proto-Germanic *walhaz (“foreigner”); alternatively from French Wallonie.

s.def: Western Sahara (a territory and former Spanish colony in North Africa; about 70% is occupied by Morocco, which claims ownership over the territory as its Southern Provinces, while 30% is governed by its native inhabitants as the Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic)

wenkbrauw.e:
From Middle Dutch wintbrauwe, from Old Dutch *wintbrāwa. The first element (Old Dutch *wint) is unknown but presumed to be related to Ancient Greek ἴονθος (íonthos). The second element (Old Dutch *brāwa) is from Proto-Germanic *brēwō.
Wenkbrauw is a doublet of wimper (“eyelash”), from a contracti...

wimper.e: From Middle Dutch wimbrauw, contraction of wintbrauwe, from Old Dutch *wintbrāwa. The first element is unknown but presumed to be related to Ancient Greek ἴονθος (íonthos); the second element is from Proto-Germanic *brēwō. Doublet of wenkbrauw (“eyebrow”). The form wimper is native in some Dutch ...

s.def: the opposite of not (used to add positive emphasis to the verb, much like the auxiliaries do or does in affirmative sentences in English)
examples: Ik dacht dat je niet van golf hield? — Ik hou wél van golf!
Je ziet wel dat...

s.def: no less than, as much as, as many as (expressing amazement)
examples: Zij heeft wel twaalf uur gewerkt vandaag!
Ik voel me wel aardig, maar niet echt goed.
Wat God doet, dat is wel gedaan.
"Dat is wel gedacht," zeide hij.

s.def: weal (general state of well-being and prosperity)
well; shaft (excavation made for the extraction of mineral resources)

wel.e: From Middle Dutch wel, from Old Dutch *wela, wala, from Proto-Germanic *wela, from Proto-Indo-European *welh₁-. Compare German wohl, English well, Icelandic vel, Swedish väl, Danish and Norwegian vel.

s.def: a female given name, masculine equivalent Willem

wilhelmina.e: From Latin Wilhelmina, feminisation of Wilhelmus.

s.def: bad, un- (preceding nouns, verbs and adjectives)

wan-.e: From Middle Dutch wan-, from Old Dutch *wan-, from Proto-Germanic *wana-, a prefixing form of *wanaz.

werpen.e:
From Middle Dutch werpen, from Old Dutch werpan, from Proto-West Germanic *werpan, from Proto-Germanic *werpaną.
Cognate with German werfen.

weit.e:
From Middle Dutch weit, weet, from Old Dutch *weit, *wēt, from Proto-West Germanic *hwaitī, from Proto-Germanic *hwaitijaz, from *hwītaz (“white”).
Cognate with Low German Weten, West Frisian weet, German Weizen, English wheat.

weduwe.e: From Middle Dutch weduwe, Old Dutch *widuwa, from Proto-West Germanic *widuwā, from Proto-Germanic *widuwǭ, from Proto-Indo-European *h₁widʰéwh₂ (“widow”). Compare German Witwe, English widow.

s.def: to whet, hone or rub on with something for the purpose of sharpening an object (typically a blade)

wetten.e: From Middle Dutch wetten, from Old Dutch *wetten, from Proto-West Germanic *hwattjan, from Proto-Germanic *hwatjaną.

s.def: to wager, to bet, to accept a gamble
examples: Je mag nooit wedden voor meer dan je je kan veroorloven te verliezen.

wedden.e:
From Middle Dutch wedden, from Old Dutch *weddon, from Frankish *weddōn, *wadjōn, from Proto-Germanic *wadjōną.
Cognate with Low German wedden, West Frisian wedzje, German wetten, English wed, Danish vædde, Icelandic veðja.

s.def: the function, duty of standing guard; a watch or vigil

wacht.e: From Middle Dutch wachte, from Old Dutch wahta, from Proto-Germanic *wahtwō.

propeller of a helicopter or similar aircraft

wiek.e: From Middle Dutch wieke, from Proto-West Germanic *weukā (“flax bundle, wick”), from Proto-Indo-European *weg- (“to weave”).

s.def: pronominal adverb form of voor + wat

examples: Ik weet niet waarvoor hij heeft gekozen.
Kun je me vertellen waarvoor dit dient?

waarvoor.e: Compare English wherefore, German wofür, Norwegian and Danish hvorfor, Swedish varför.

examples: Hij wendde zich tot de overheid.
Klaar om te wenden? Ree!

wenden.e: From Middle Dutch wenden, from Old Dutch wenden, from Proto-West Germanic *wandijan, from Proto-Germanic *wandijaną. Historically a causative verb to winden (“to wind”).

s.def: desert (environmental area with very little precipitation)

woestijn.e: From Middle Dutch woestine, from Old Dutch wuostinna, from Proto-West Germanic *wōstini. Cognate with German Wüste, English waste.

examples: een wisse dood

wis.e: Derived from Proto-Germanic *wissaz, past participle of *witaną.

wrang.e: From Middle Dutch wranc, from Old Dutch *wrang, from Proto-Germanic *wrangaz.

weite.e: First attested as Weite in 1913. Etymology uncertain. The toponym may derive from a hydronym or, alternatively, be a reference to the cultivation of buckwheat (boekweit) near the settlement.

wafel.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch wafel, wafele, wavel, from Old Dutch *wāvila, from Proto-Germanic *wēbilǭ, *wēbilō, possibly related to Proto-Indo-European *webʰ- (“to braid, weave”) (whence Dutch weven (“to weave”) and English weave).

s.def: field of interest and action, chapter on a to do list

wellen.e: From Middle Dutch wellen, Old Dutch *wellen, from Proto-West Germanic *wallijan.

wesp.e:
From earlier Modern Dutch wespe, from Middle Dutch wespe, from Old Dutch *wespa, with metathesis from Proto-West Germanic *wapsu, from Proto-Germanic *wepsō-, from Proto-Indo-European *wóbʰseh₂ (“wasp”), from *webʰ- (“weave”).
Cognate with German Wespe, Low German Wösp, English wasp.

wedergeboorte.e: From Middle Dutch wedergeboorte. Equivalent to weder (“again”) + geboorte (“birth”).

wedstrijd.e: From wed (“bet”) + strijd (“fight”), as a calque of German Wettstreit (“competition”). The German word has never been common in the sense of an organised match or contest, however. This sense, therefore, must have developed in Dutch either independently or as a calque of another German word, name...

waarheid.e: From Middle Dutch waerhede, from Old Dutch wārheit, wārheid, wārheide, from Proto-West Germanic *wārahaidu. Equivalent to waar + -heid.

to wind (turn coils of something around)

winden.e: From Middle Dutch winden, from Old Dutch *windan, from Proto-Germanic *windaną.

s.def: a hole in ice (on the surface of a body of water)

wak.e: From Middle Dutch wac. Related to wake, from Old Dutch *waka, from Proto-Germanic *wakwō. Probably not identical to this form, however, as both the gender and formation are different: wake and its cognates are feminine ō-stems, while wac is a neuter a-stem. waterloo.e:

* (Utrecht) Named after Waterloo in Belgium in memory of the Battle of Waterloo. The choice of name may have been influenced by nearby Austerlitz.
* (Zeeland) First attested as Waterloo in 1853. Named...

watering.e: From Middle Dutch wateringe, from Old Dutch weteringa. Equivalent to wateren + -ing.

wilg.e: From Middle Dutch wilge, from Old Dutch *wilga, from Proto-West Germanic *wiligijā.

willis.e: Derived from a dialectal variant of wildernis (“wilderness”).

weet.e: From Middle Dutch wete. See the verb weten (“to know”).

wil.e: From Middle Dutch wille, from Old Dutch willo, from Proto-West Germanic *willjō, from Proto-Germanic *wiljô.

warenhuis.e: From waren (“goods”) + huis (“house”), calque of German Warenhaus.

waarschuwen.e: From Middle Dutch waerschuwen, waerschouwen, waerscuwen, waerscouwen, equivalent to (ge)waar + schuwen. Cognate with nautical German wahrschauen.

examples: En dan krijg je nog wisselgeld.

wisselgeld.e: From Middle Dutch wisselgelt (“fee for converting to a different currency”). Equivalent to wisselen + geld, or less probably wissel + geld.

to vary, to change (over time, by circumstances etc.)

wisselen.e: From Middle Dutch wisselen, from Old Dutch *wissalon, from Proto-Germanic *wihslōną. Equivalent to wissel + -en.

s.def: waterbed (mattress filled with water; bed with such a mattress)
examples: In de jaren '80 waren waterbedden erg populair.

s.def: to wax (remove hairs by pulling off a thin film of wax)
turn of events, change of circumstance

wonen.e: From Middle Dutch wōnen, from Old Dutch wonon, from Proto-West Germanic *wunēn, from Proto-Germanic *wunāną, from Proto-Indo-European *wenh₁- (“to wish, love”).

pronominal adverb form of aan + wat
pronominal adverb form of van + wat; of which

woede.e: From Middle Dutch woet, from Old Dutch *wuot, *wuodi, from Proto-Germanic *wōþuz. Compare English wode.

what is the problem at hand? What is going on?
pronominal adverb form of op + wat

woning.e: From Middle Dutch woninge. Equivalent to wonen (“to live, reside”) + -ing.

examples: Deze oude munt heeft een hoge waarde voor verzamelaars.

waarde.e: By surface analysis, waard (“value”, pedicative adjective) + -e (abstract noun suffix). From Middle Dutch werde which still has the expected i-mutation (compare kou, hulde), from Proto-West Germanic *werþī f. In Middle Dutch the forms warde, waerde arose under influence of the adjective. Cognate ...

s.def: maxim, saying, adage, concise nugget of wisdom
examples: In haar boeken deelt ze vele wijsheden over geluk en persoonlijke groei.
Met de jaren vergaarde hij veel wijsheden over het leven.

wijsheid.e: From Middle Dutch wijsheit, from Old Dutch wīshēt. By surface analysis, wijs + -heid.

examples: Wijs niet naar anderen, dat is onbeleefd!
De pijl wijst in de richting van de dichtstbijzijnde nooduitgang.
De klok wijst twaalf uur.
De leraar wees de leerlingen op hun fouten.

s.def: to direct to (by extension, to send to)
examples: Kunt u mij de toiletten wijzen alstublieft?
De onderhandelingen waren op niets uitgelopen, en de diplomaten werden de deur gewezen.

wijzen.e: From Middle Dutch wisen, from Old Dutch wīsen, from Proto-West Germanic *wīsijan, from Proto-Germanic *wīsijaną.

s.def: pronominal adverb form of in + wat

examples: Ik word altijd wakker met een wijsje in mijn hoofd. — I always wake up with a little tune in my head. (Kinderen voor Kinderen, "Wakker met een wijsje")

s.def: real, actual (having a basis in true events) (often used of stories, including fictionalised ones)

s.def: second-person (gij) singular past indicative of wijzen

Ik waardeer haar gezang zeer, het houdt wilde dieren op afstand.

Het is belangrijk om de waarde van je eigendommen te waarderen voor verzekeringen.

waarderen.e: From Middle Dutch werderen. Equivalent to waarde (“value, worth”) + -eren.

webwinkel.e: From web (“World Wide Web”) + winkel (“shop, store”).

examples: Je kunt je profielinformatie op elk moment wijzigen in de instellingen van de app.
De leraar vroeg de studenten om enkele zinnen in hun opstel te wijzigen.

wijzigen.e: From wijze (“method”) + -ig + -en; calque of Latin modificare.

examples: Hij houdt zijn werkplek altijd schoon en opgeruimd.
De werkplek is uitgerust met alle benodigde apparatuur.

wensen.e: From Middle Dutch wenschen, also wonschen, wunschen in the eastern dialects, from Old Dutch *wunsken, from Proto-Germanic *wunskijaną.

s.def: pronominal adverb form of bij + wat; whereby

examples: Het kapotte horloge was nu waardeloos, omdat het niet meer te repareren was.
De neppe schilderijen bleken waardelozer te zijn dan ze aanvankelijk dachten.

examples: Dat geld is niets waard.

waard.e: From Middle Dutch wāert, wēert, from Old Dutch *wert, from Proto-Germanic *werduz.

winkelwagen.e: From winkel (“shop”) + wagen (“cart, wagon”) or from winkelen (“to shop”) + wagen (“cart, wagon”).

wijken.e: From Middle Dutch wiken, from Old Dutch *wīcan, from Proto-West Germanic *wīkwan, from Proto-Germanic *wīkwaną.

wrijven.e: From Middle Dutch wriven, from Old Dutch *wrīvan, from Proto-West Germanic *wrīban.

examples: Hij legde de wijze van berekening uit aan zijn studenten.
Op welke wijze kunnen we dit probleem oplossen?
De oude wijze zat onder de boom en gaf advies aan zijn toehoorders.
Volgens de legende kwamen de drie wijzen uit het oosten om het pasgeboren kind te bezoeken.

wonden.e: From Middle Dutch wonden, from Middle Dutch *wundon, from Proto-West Germanic *wundōn, from Proto-Germanic *wundōną. By surface analysis, wond + -en.

wond.e: From Middle Dutch wonde, wunde, from Old Dutch wunda, from Proto-Germanic *wundō. Compare Low German Wunn, German Wunde, West Frisian wûne, English wound, Icelandic und.

examples: De waarnemer noteerde alle details van het experiment.
Hij is de waarnemer van onze organisatie bij de conferentie.
Zijn waarneming van de gebeurtenissen was zeer gedetailleerd.
Kleur is een waarneming van het oog.

examples: Ik kan de geur van vers gebakken brood waarnemen.

s.def: to be acting, to temporarily assume the duties of another person
examples: De vice-president zal de taken van de president waarnemen tijdens zijn afwezigheid.

waarnemen.e: From Middle Dutch waernēmen. By surface analysis, waar (“care, attention”) + nemen; compare waarloos.

s.def: secondary canal in a turf extraction area

wijk.e:
From Middle Dutch wijc, from Old Dutch *wīk, from Proto-West Germanic *wīk, from Latin vīcus (“hamlet, village, neighborhood”), from Proto-Indo-European *weyḱ- (“village, household”).
Cognate with English wick, -wich, -wick, German Weich- in dated Weichbild (“municipal area”). Compare Gothic 𐍅𐌴𐌹𐌷...

whale, mammal of the order Cetacea

walvis.e: From Middle Dutch walvisch, from Old Dutch walvisk, from *wal (“whale”), presumably to clarify the meaning. Equivalent to wal (“whale”) + vis (“fish”). Old Dutch *wal is from Proto-West Germanic *hwal, from Proto-Germanic *hwalaz, from Proto-Indo-European *(s)kʷálos (“sheatfish”). See also waldie...

examples: De wasberen leven vooral in de Ardennen.

wasbeer.e: From wassen (“to wash”) + beer (“bear”). Probably a calque of German Waschbär, itself a calque of translingual Ursus lotor.

a hamlet in Wijchen, Gelderland, Netherlands

wezel.e: From Middle Dutch wēsel, from Old Dutch *wisula, *wisala, from Proto-West Germanic *wisulā, from Proto-Germanic *wisulǭ.

examples: Na de laatste wijziging van het document waren alle fouten gecorrigeerd.
De software-update bracht enkele belangrijke wijzigingen in de gebruikersinterface.

s.def: pronominal adverb form of na + wat
second/third-person singular dependent-clause present indicative of welvaren

welvaart.e: From Middle Dutch welvaert, equivalent to wel + vaart. Compare West Frisian wolfeart, Middle Low German wolvart, German Wohlfart. Compare also Middle Dutch welvare. More at English welfare.

s.def: a wedge-shaped object, like a bread
wheat bread, often a special type of bread, with great regional differences in meaning

wegge.e: Alternative form of wig. From Middle Dutch wegge.

examples: De werknemer heeft recht op vakantiedagen.
